(25) Learning Hexo from Scratch - SEO: Extra Concepts
Last post in the SEO chapter. SEO is a deep topic, so this post just rounds out a few extra concepts.
I've covered a bunch of SEO tweaks in earlier posts, but those are all surface-level. Have you ever thought about what Google actually
Say you searched "JavaScript" today — how does Google find the result you're looking for out of all the data out there? Quick tour of how search engines work.
A search engine is kind of like an online library. So when we search "JavaScript" and the result page says something like "

", how does the engine know which one you'd actually want to see? That's where search ranking comes in — and how does ranking work? That gets into algorithms: which URL gets the most clicks, which sites match the user's keywords, which results are trending, even whether the user has visited a particular site before. These all feed into the ranking, which is why SEO is such a deep field — there's a massive algorithm behind it. So what is Google doing for us? Basically pulling out what we most want to see from a mountain of data, including the article content.
There are lots of ways to boost SEO. One interesting one is adding a
meta tag — though Google has stated that
"The Meta Keyword Tag Is No Longer Used by Google in 2018"
. A lot of sites still add this field, but it's not part of Google's ranking algorithm.
NexT will automatically add the keyword based on the tags you provide.
People often talk about semantic HTML — using HTML5 semantic tags like

, etc. But when you pick a theme, the theme should handle all this for you. So tag usage usually isn't something you need to worry about. What you should actually worry about is whether the theme is being maintained and whether they're optimising the structure for SEO.
Loading speed is another Google ranking signal. If your page loads slowly, your SEO suffers. So things like CDN load count and rendering performance also matter.
If you're not sure how fast your blog loads, give Google's official
Here's my own blog as the example:
These are just for reference — pushing the mobile score to 100 is genuinely hard.

With smartphones being everywhere now, Google's ranking also factors in whether a site is mobile-friendly. But like I mentioned, a good theme handles all of this for you. Side note — I seem to recall Google saying mobile signals weigh heavier these days (memory's fuzzy).
After all that talk, the takeaway is: most SEO problems are handled by the theme you pick. So what's left for SEO?
If your articles are substantive and useful, you'll naturally climb the rankings. So when you're writing blog posts, my advice is just to take it as it comes — write what you want to write.
